name: Apples of Hesperides
description: The Apples of the Hesperides are golden apples that grow in the Garden of the Hesperides, a mythical orchard located at the edge of the world. The garden is guarded by the Hesperides, nymphs who are daughters of Atlas, and a hundred-headed dragon named Ladon. These apples are not ordinary fruit; they possess the power of immortality and eternal youth. Gaia, the primordial Earth goddess, gifted the apple tree to Hera as a wedding present when she married Zeus. Hera, entrusting the Hesperides with their care, also appointed Ladon to ensure their safety. Obtaining these apples was the eleventh labor of Heracles. He had to either trick Atlas into retrieving the apples while Heracles held up the heavens in his stead, or slay Ladon and pluck the apples himself. The apples are a symbol of immortality, divine power, and the challenges one must overcome to attain such gifts. In some accounts, Athena returned the apples to the Hesperides after Heracles completed his labor, reinforcing their sacred nature and the importance of their protected location. The golden apples were a prize worth risking one's life for, representing the ultimate reward of eternal life and youth.
subtitle: Golden fruit of immortality and youth.
origin: Gifted by Gaia to Hera as a wedding present.
currentLocation: Mythical Garden of the Hesperides (unknown location)

keyMyths.title: The Eleventh Labor of Heracles
keyMyths.description: Heracles was tasked with retrieving the Apples of the Hesperides as his eleventh labor. He either tricked Atlas or slew Ladon to obtain them.

keyMyths.title: The Wedding Gift
keyMyths.description: Gaia gifted the golden apple tree to Hera as a wedding present when she married Zeus, symbolizing a sacred and powerful union.

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
